9: A direct revelation: 'Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion! Shout aloud, O daughter of Jerusalem! Behold, your king is coming to you; righteous and having salvation is he, humble and mounted on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey.' The triumphal entry is one of the most deliberately staged Christological events in the Gospels: Jesus specifically arranges for the donkey (Luke 19:30-34), enters Jerusalem in a way that fulfills Zechariah's vision exactly, and Matthew and John both cite the fulfillment (Matthew 21:5; John 12:15). The theological content of the sign: a king on a war horse signals military conquest; a king on a donkey signals peace and humility. The Messiah comes not to destroy enemies with military power but to bring salvation through the humble, peaceable means of his own sacrifice. The crowds' hosannas (Psalms 118:26, 'Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD') are their recognition of the sign, even if they misread its implications.
1: The word massa’ (oracle/burden) introduces a new section — chapters 9-11, sometimes called Deutero-Zechariah. The oracle sweeps north to south through Aram, Phoenicia, and Philistia, recapitulating YHWH’s judgment on the nations. The phrase “the eyes of all humanity and of all Israel are fixed on the LORD” sets the eschatological tone: universal attention converges on YHWH’s action. The arc concludes in Zechariah 9:9 with the coming king — the ultimate focus of all eyes. John 12:32 echoes this: “I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself.”
2: Hamath on the northern horizon and Tyre and Sidon on the coast are proverbial for wisdom and power (Ezek 28 personifies Tyre’s prince as the pinnacle of wisdom). Yet their cleverness does not preserve them from divine judgment. The NT parallel: “Has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?” (1 Corinthians 1:20). Human wisdom and commercial power (Tyre = trading empire) are not ultimate; the kingdom that survives belongs to the humble king of Zechariah 9:9.
3: Tyre’s silver like dust and gold like mud depict extreme wealth — and extreme attachment to material security. The fortress (matzor) she builds for herself becomes ironic: the one who trusts in walls and wealth is most exposed. Jesus’ parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) and his warning about mammon (Matthew 6:24) echo this irony. The wealth of Tyre passes; the kingdom of the meek king (Zechariah 9:9) endures.
4: YHWH will dispossess Tyre and destroy her wealth. Alexander the Great’s 332 BCE siege is commonly cited as historical fulfillment: he built a causeway using rubble thrown into the sea, technically fulfilling Ezekiel 26:12. The verse connects to a broader pattern: worldly power built on wealth and walls falls before YHWH’s sovereign action. Rev 18’s lament over Babylon/Rome recapitulates this judgment pattern in apocalyptic terms, anticipating the destruction of all economic empires opposed to God.
5: The Philistine cities — Ashkelon, Gaza, Ekron — tremble at Tyre’s fall. Their reaction: terror, writhing, withered hope. The loss of Gaza’s king signals political collapse. These coastal cities represent the residual Canaanite/Philistine opposition to Israel that has persisted since the conquest. Their judgment clears the way for the restored people under the coming king. Acts 8:26-40 places Philip’s ministry to the Ethiopian eunuch on the Gaza road — the gospel reaches regions once under Philistine control.
6: A “mixed people” (mamzer, of uncertain etymology — possibly “illegitimate mixing” or simply “foreigners”) settles Ashdod as Philistia’s pride is cut off. The mixed settlement signals ethnic dissolution: national identities that opposed Israel lose their coherence under divine judgment. This points forward to Pentecost’s multinational gathering (Acts 2) and Paul’s declaration that in Christ there is neither Jew nor Greek (Galatians 3:28) — though the context there is inclusion, not judgment.
7: The remarkable promise: even the Philistines (remnant of the Philistines) can become “like a clan within Judah,” and Ekron like the Jebusites (incorporated into David’s Jerusalem). Blood from their mouths and forbidden things from between their teeth may refer to dietary laws — their incorporation involves covenant conformity. This is the OT precedent for Gentile inclusion without ethnic erasure. Ephesians 2:19 declares former Gentiles “no longer strangers and foreigners, but fellow citizens” — the Jebusites-in-Jerusalem logic scaled to the world.
8: YHWH stations himself as a guard around his house so that no army passes through again. The phrase ki ’attāh rā’îtî beinay (“for now I am watching with my own eyes”) is a sovereign claim of personal oversight. The “no more passing through” is reminiscent of the Passover: the destroyer passed over guarded houses. John 10:28-29 articulates the security theme Christocentrically: no one can snatch believers from Christ’s hand or the Father’s hand. The divine garrison is now the Good Shepherd.
10: The king of Zechariah 9:9 disarms the war machine: chariot, war horse, battle bow are cut off. His reign is accomplished by disarmament, not conquest. Ve-dibber shalom la-goyim (“he will speak peace to the nations”) — the king’s word is the instrument of universal peace. His dominion: “from sea to sea, from the River to the ends of the earth” echoes Psalms 72:8 (the Solomonic ideal). Ephesians 2:14-17 identifies Christ as “our peace,” who by his cross broke down the dividing wall and “preached peace to those who were far off and peace to those who were near.”
11: “Because of the blood of my covenant with you, I will free your prisoners from the waterless pit.” The “blood of the covenant” (dam beritekh) echoes Exodus 24:8 (Moses sprinkling covenant blood at Sinai) and is directly applied by Jesus at the Last Supper: “This is my blood of the covenant” (Matthew 26:28; Mark 14:24). The waterless pit (bor ein bo mayim) recalls Joseph (Genesis 37:24) and Jeremiah (Jeremiah 38:6) — the type of rescue from death. Christ’s resurrection is the ultimate liberation from the pit.
12: “Return to the stronghold (lā-bitzarôn), you prisoners of hope.” Assirei ha-tiqvāh (prisoners of hope) is a striking phrase: prisoners whose very captivity is oriented toward hope. The double restoration (“I will restore double”) echoes Job 42:10-12 and Isaiah 61:7. Jesus’ proclamation at Nazareth (Luke 4:18-19, citing Isa 61) — “to set at liberty those who are oppressed” — addresses exactly these prisoners of hope. The bitzarôn (stronghold/fortress) into which they return is now the refuge that is Christ (Ps 46; Hebrews 6:18-19).
13: YHWH bends Judah as a bow and makes Ephraim the arrow, wielded against the sons of Greece (Yāvān). Many scholars read this as a reference to the Maccabean resistance to Hellenism (167-164 BCE), making it one of the latest dateable allusions in the Prophets. Christologically, the conflict between covenant people and Hellenistic cultural pressure prefigures the church’s struggle in a Greco-Roman world. Revelation 19:11-15 depicts the return of Christ himself as the divine warrior — sword from his mouth replacing the bow and arrow of Zechariah 9:13.
14: YHWH’s theophanic appearance: he will appear (yera’eh) over them, his arrow like lightning, the trumpet sounding, advancing in storm. This is the divine warrior storm theophany drawn from the exodus-Sinai tradition (Ps 18; Hab 3). Lightning and trumpet appear together in Sinai (Exodus 19:16) and in the NT’s parousia texts: “For the Lord himself will descend from heaven with a cry of command, with the voice of an archangel, and with the sound of the trumpet of God” (1 Thessalonians 4:16; cf. Matthew 24:31).
15: The LORD of Hosts shields his people; they devour their enemies and are “full as a sacrificial basin, drenched like the corners of the altar.” The imagery of altar-drenching evokes the slaughter offerings at the sanctuary. Paradoxically, the warriors become like priests offering sacrifice — the boundary between battle and liturgy dissolves. In Rev 19, Christ’s victory is followed by “the great supper of God” (Zechariah 9:17) — apocalyptic feast imagery that draws on this sacrificial-battle complex.
16: “The LORD their God will save them as his people’s flock.” The shepherd-flock-saving complex culminates in the simile: they will “gleam like gems (avnei nezer, coronation stones) in a crown over his land.” The people become jewels in YHWH’s crown — a royal image of dignity and worth. Malachi 3:17 uses the same image (precious possession); 1 Peter 2:9 calls the church “a royal priesthood, a holy nation” — the crown-gem status of Zechariah 9:16 applied ecclesially. Ephesians 1:18 speaks of the riches of God’s “glorious inheritance in the saints.”
17: The doxological close: “How great is his goodness, how great is his beauty!” (mah tuvô umah yopiyô). The goodness and beauty of YHWH overflow into agricultural blessing (grain, new wine), a synecdoche for total shalom. Psalms 27:4 uses similar language for YHWH’s beauty (yophî). In the NT, the beauty and goodness of God are revealed supremely in the face of Jesus Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6: “the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ”). The eschatological feast (Revelation 19:9; Matthew 8:11) materializes the grain-and-wine blessing.
